rawText:
The question in this title is deliberately broad, because the broadest questions are often the ones we avoid asking — not because they lack answers, but because any honest answer forces us to confront the distance between our stated values and our actual behavior.

Most people, if asked, would say they want a world that is fair, peaceful, sustainable, and kind — yet the systems we collectively maintain often produce outcomes that are none of those things, and we participate in those systems every day without much protest.

This is not hypocrisy in the simple sense; it is the more complicated reality that individual choices operate within structures that constrain what any single person can accomplish.

A consumer who cares about the environment still drives to work because the city was designed around cars; a voter who values equity still benefits from arrangements that disadvantage others, often without being fully aware of it.

Recognizing this tension is the starting point of honest moral thinking, because it replaces the comfortable fiction of personal purity with the harder question of collective responsibility.

If the world we want cannot be built by individual virtue alone, then the conversation must turn to institutions — the rules, norms, and organizations through which societies make collective decisions.

Good institutions do not require every participant to be selfless; they align incentives so that ordinary, self-interested behavior produces outcomes that are broadly acceptable.

A well-designed tax system, for instance, does not depend on citizens voluntarily donating money to public services; it creates a structure in which contribution is expected and the burden is shared.

The same logic applies to environmental regulation, public education, health care, and every other domain where private interest and public good must be reconciled.

Building better institutions requires something that pure idealism often lacks: patience with imperfection, willingness to compromise, and the understanding that incremental progress, however unsatisfying, is usually more durable than dramatic revolution.

History suggests that the societies that improve most reliably are not those that demand perfection but those that create feedback loops — elections, free press, independent courts — through which mistakes can be identified and corrected without tearing the whole system apart.

The world we want to build, then, is not a utopia but a learning system: one that is honest about its failures, responsive to evidence, and committed to the principle that every generation should leave conditions slightly better than it found them.

That principle sounds modest, but consistently applied over decades, it produces transformations that no single visionary could have planned.

The question is not whether we can imagine a perfect world — anyone can do that — but whether we are willing to do the patient, unglamorous work of making the actual world a little more like the one we imagine.

overview.title: What Kind of World Do We Want to Build?

sentences.text: Most people want a world that is fair and kind, but the world we live in is not always like that.
sentences.translation: 대부분의 사람들은 공정하고 친절한 세상을 원하지만, 우리가 사는 세상이 항상 그렇지는 않아요.

sentences.text: One person alone cannot fix everything because the systems around us are very big and complicated.
sentences.translation: 한 사람만으로는 모든 것을 고칠 수 없어요. 왜냐하면 우리 주변의 시스템이 매우 크고 복잡하기 때문이에요.

sentences.text: We need good rules and organizations that help everyone work together to make things better.
sentences.translation: 우리는 모두가 함께 일하며 더 나은 것을 만들도록 돕는 좋은 규칙과 조직이 필요해요.

sentences.text: Making the world better takes a lot of patience because big changes happen slowly, not all at once.
sentences.translation: 세상을 더 좋게 만드는 것은 큰 인내가 필요해요. 왜냐하면 큰 변화는 한꺼번에가 아니라 천천히 일어나기 때문이에요.

sentences.text: The best societies are not perfect ones, but ones that learn from their mistakes and keep improving.
sentences.translation: 가장 좋은 사회는 완벽한 사회가 아니라 실수에서 배우고 계속 개선하는 사회예요.

sentences.text: The real question is whether we are willing to do small, everyday work to make our world a little bit better.
sentences.translation: 진짜 질문은 우리가 세상을 조금 더 낫게 만들기 위한 작고 일상적인 노력을 할 의향이 있느냐는 거예요.

sentences.text: The essay asks why there is a gap between the values people claim to hold and the way the world actually operates.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 사람들이 주장하는 가치관과 세상이 실제로 작동하는 방식 사이에 왜 격차가 있는지를 묻고 있어요.

sentences.text: Individual choices are limited by larger systems, so personal virtue alone cannot produce a just society.
sentences.translation: 개인의 선택은 더 큰 시스템에 의해 제한되므로, 개인의 미덕만으로는 정의로운 사회를 만들 수 없어요.

sentences.text: The author argues that well-designed institutions align self-interest with the public good, reducing the need for individual sacrifice.
sentences.translation: 저자는 잘 설계된 제도가 사익과 공익을 일치시켜 개인의 희생 필요성을 줄인다고 주장해요.

sentences.text: Incremental progress through feedback mechanisms like elections and free press is more durable than revolutionary change.
sentences.translation: 선거와 언론의 자유 같은 피드백 메커니즘을 통한 점진적 진보가 혁명적 변화보다 더 지속적이에요.

sentences.text: The ideal is not a utopia but a learning system that corrects mistakes and responds to evidence over time.
sentences.translation: 이상은 유토피아가 아니라 실수를 교정하고 시간이 지남에 따라 증거에 반응하는 학습 시스템이에요.

sentences.text: The essay concludes that building a better world requires patient, unglamorous effort rather than grand visions of perfection.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 더 나은 세상을 만들려면 완벽한 웅장한 비전이 아니라 인내심 있고 화려하지 않은 노력이 필요하다고 결론짓고 있어요.

sentences.text: This synthesis essay employs a deliberative rhetorical strategy, posing a broad moral question and then systematically narrowing it through institutional analysis.
sentences.translation: 이 종합적 에세이는 숙의적 수사 전략을 사용하여 광범위한 도덕적 질문을 제기한 뒤 제도적 분석을 통해 체계적으로 좁혀 나가요.

sentences.text: The writer transitions from individual ethics to structural critique, arguing that personal purity is a "comfortable fiction" that obscures collective responsibility.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 개인 윤리에서 구조적 비판으로 전환하며, 개인적 순수함이 집단적 책임을 가리는 "편안한 허구"라고 주장해요.

sentences.text: Abstract nouns like "incentives," "institutions," and "reconciliation" carry the conceptual weight, demanding sustained analytical reading.
sentences.translation: "인센티브", "제도", "화해" 같은 추상 명사가 개념적 무게를 지탱하며, 지속적인 분석적 독해를 요구해요.

sentences.text: The essay balances idealism with pragmatism through hedging constructions — "however unsatisfying," "in many cases" — maintaining intellectual honesty.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 "아무리 불만족스럽더라도", "많은 경우에" 같은 완화 구문을 통해 이상주의와 실용주의의 균형을 맞추며 지적 정직성을 유지해요.

sentences.text: Semicolons and colons structure multi-clause arguments, connecting independent propositions into unified chains of reasoning characteristic of advanced persuasive prose.
sentences.translation: 세미콜론과 콜론이 다중 절 논증을 구조화하여, 독립적 명제들을 고급 설득적 산문에 특징적인 통합된 추론 사슬로 연결해요.

sentences.text: The concluding rhetorical question reframes grand ambition as humble practice, resolving the essay's central tension between vision and action.
sentences.translation: 결론부의 수사적 질문은 거대한 야심을 겸손한 실천으로 재구성하여, 비전과 행동 사이의 에세이 핵심 긴장을 해소해요.

sentences.original: The question in this title is deliberately broad, because the broadest questions are often the ones we avoid asking — not because they lack answers, but because any honest answer forces us to confront the distance between our stated values and our actual behavior.
sentences.translation: 이 제목의 질문은 의도적으로 광범위한데, 가장 광범위한 질문은 흔히 우리가 묻기를 회피하는 것들이기 때문이다 — 답이 없어서가 아니라, 어떤 정직한 답이든 우리가 선언한 가치와 실제 행동 사이의 거리를 직면하게 만들기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: because the broadest questions are often the ones we avoid asking
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (최상급 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: not because they lack answers, but because any honest answer forces us to confront the distance between our stated values and our actual behavior
grammarElements.label: not A but B 대조절 (이중 because)

vocabulary.example: The question was deliberately left open.
We must confront uncomfortable truths.
There is often a gap between stated values and actual practice.

expressions.usage: 관계절로 특정 유형의 질문을 지칭하며 회피 경향을 암시한다

expressions.phrase: the distance between A and B
expressions.usage: 두 요소 사이의 괴리를 공간적 비유로 표현할 때 쓴다

patterns.pattern: [Subject] is deliberately [adjective], because [reason] — not because [A], but because [B]
patterns.explanation: 의도적 선택의 이유를 밝히되, "not A but B" 구조로 피상적 이유를 부정하고 진짜 이유를 제시하는 도입 패턴. 에세이의 자기 인식적 시작을 보여준다.
examples: This definition is deliberately vague, because the vaguest definitions are the hardest to reject — not because they cover everything, but because any precise version forces us to choose what to exclude.
The title is deliberately simple, because the simplest phrases are the ones we think we understand — not because they are easy, but because any careful reading reveals layers we overlooked.

drillQuestions.question: The question in this title is deliberately _____.
We avoid asking broad questions not because they lack _____.
Any honest answer forces us to _____ the distance between values and behavior.
"Deliberately" means done on _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 자기 반영적 도입부. 제목 자체를 메타적으로 분석하며 시작한다. "not because A, but because B" 이중 because 구조가 피상적 이유와 진짜 이유를 분리한다. "stated values"와 "actual behavior"의 대비가 글 전체의 핵심 긴장을 예고한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The question in this title is deliberately broad
because the broadest questions are often the ones we avoid asking
koreanAlignment.ko: 가장 광범위한 질문은 흔히 우리가 묻기를 회피하는 것들이기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: not because they lack answers
but because any honest answer forces us to confront
koreanAlignment.ko: 어떤 정직한 답이든 직면하게 만들기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: the distance between our stated values and our actual behavior
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 선언한 가치와 실제 행동 사이의 거리를

pronunciationNotes: deliberately: /dɪˈlɪbərətli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "딜리버릿리"에 가깝다. -ately가 빠르게 축약된다.
confront: /kənˈfrʌnt/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컨프런트"에 가깝다. fr을 한 번에 빠르게 발음.

sentences.original: Most people, if asked, would say they want a world that is fair, peaceful, sustainable, and kind — yet the systems we collectively maintain often produce outcomes that are none of those things, and we participate in those systems every day without much protest.
sentences.translation: 대부분의 사람들은, 질문을 받으면, 공정하고 평화롭고 지속 가능하며 친절한 세상을 원한다고 말할 것이다 — 그러나 우리가 집단적으로 유지하는 시스템은 종종 그 어느 것도 아닌 결과를 생산하며, 우리는 매일 별다른 항의 없이 그 시스템에 참여한다.

grammarElements.text: they want a world that is fair, peaceful, sustainable, and kind
grammarElements.label: 목적어 명사절 (관계절 포함 4중 병렬)

grammarElements.text: yet the systems we collectively maintain often produce outcomes that are none of those things
grammarElements.label: yet 역접절 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: and we participate in those systems every day without much protest
grammarElements.label: 병렬절 (전치사구)

vocabulary.example: A sustainable world requires responsible resource use.
We collectively decide how society runs.
They accepted the rules without much protest.

expressions.usage: 주어와 동사 사이에 삽입된 축약 조건절. "if they were asked"의 축약형
앞서 나열한 항목을 모두 부정할 때 쓰는 간결한 표현

patterns.pattern: Most people would say [ideal] — yet [reality], and we [participate] without [resistance]
patterns.explanation: 이상과 현실의 괴리를 대시와 yet으로 극적으로 대비하고, 무저항적 참여를 덧붙여 공모의 문제를 제기하는 패턴.
examples: Most citizens would say they value honesty — yet the institutions they support routinely hide information, and they accept it without questioning.
Most consumers would say they care about workers — yet the products they buy are made under exploitative conditions, and they purchase them without hesitation.

drillQuestions.question: Most people would say they want a world that is fair, peaceful, sustainable, and _____.
The systems we collectively maintain produce outcomes that are _____ of those things.
We participate in those systems without much _____.
"If asked" is a shortened form of "if they _____ asked."

sentences.notes: 이상과 현실의 괴리를 극적으로 제시하는 핵심 문장. "if asked"라는 삽입 축약절이 가정적 상황을 간결히 설정한다. "fair, peaceful, sustainable, and kind"의 4중 병렬과 "none of those things"의 전면 부정이 강한 대비를 만든다. "without much protest"가 공모의 뉘앙스를 추가한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Most people, if asked, would say
koreanAlignment.ko: 대부분의 사람들은, 질문을 받으면, 말할 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: they want a world that is fair, peaceful, sustainable, and kind
koreanAlignment.ko: 공정하고 평화롭고 지속 가능하며 친절한 세상을 원한다고

koreanAlignment.en: yet the systems we collectively maintain
often produce outcomes that are none of those things
koreanAlignment.ko: 종종 그 어느 것도 아닌 결과를 생산하며

koreanAlignment.en: and we participate in those systems every day without much protest
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리는 매일 별다른 항의 없이 그 시스템에 참여한다

pronunciationNotes: sustainable: /səˈsteɪnəbəl/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "서스테이너블"에 가깝다.
collectively: /kəˈlektɪvli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컬렉티블리"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: This is not hypocrisy in the simple sense; it is the more complicated reality that individual choices operate within structures that constrain what any single person can accomplish.
sentences.translation: 이것은 단순한 의미의 위선이 아니다; 이것은 개인의 선택이 어떤 한 사람이 성취할 수 있는 것을 제약하는 구조 안에서 작동한다는 더 복잡한 현실이다.

grammarElements.text: it is the more complicated reality
grammarElements.label: 세미콜론 뒤 병렬절 (비교급 명사구)

grammarElements.text: that individual choices operate within structures that constrain what any single person can accomplish
grammarElements.label: 동격 that절 (이중 관계절)

vocabulary.example: Accusing others of hypocrisy is easy; examining our own is harder.
Structures constrain what individuals can do.
No single person can accomplish systemic change alone.

expressions.phrase: not X in the simple sense
expressions.usage: 표면적 해석을 부정하고 더 깊은 분석으로 전환할 때 쓰는 학술적 표현
개인의 행동이 시스템적 맥락에 의해 제한됨을 나타내는 사회과학적 표현

patterns.pattern: This is not [simple label]; it is the more complicated reality that [structural explanation]
patterns.explanation: 세미콜론으로 단순한 판단을 부정하고 더 복잡한 구조적 설명을 제시하는 패턴. 학술적 글쓰기에서 뉘앙스를 추가하는 핵심 전략이다.
examples: This is not laziness in the simple sense; it is the more complicated reality that motivation depends on systems that either support or undermine effort.
This is not failure in the ordinary sense; it is the more complicated reality that success requires conditions that not everyone has access to.

drillQuestions.question: This is not _____ in the simple sense.
Individual choices operate within _____ that constrain what people can do.
"Constrain" means to _____ what is possible.
The semicolon separates a simple explanation from a more _____ one.

sentences.notes: 세미콜론이 두 개의 독립절을 연결하며, 단순한 판단("위선")을 부정하고 구조적 분석으로 전환한다. "operate within structures that constrain"이라는 사회과학적 언어가 글의 분석적 깊이를 보여준다. 이중 관계절(structures that constrain what ~ can accomplish)이 복합적 종속 구조를 만든다.

koreanAlignment.en: This is not hypocrisy in the simple sense
it is the more complicated reality
that individual choices operate within structures
that constrain what any single person can accomplish
koreanAlignment.ko: 어떤 한 사람이 성취할 수 있는 것을 제약하는

pronunciationNotes: hypocrisy: /hɪˈpɑːkrəsi/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "히파크러시"에 가깝다. y는 /ɪ/로 발음.
constrain: /kənˈstreɪn/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "컨스트레인"에 가깝다. str을 한 번에 발음.

sentences.original: A consumer who cares about the environment still drives to work because the city was designed around cars; a voter who values equity still benefits from arrangements that disadvantage others, often without being fully aware of it.
sentences.translation: 환경을 걱정하는 소비자도 도시가 자동차 중심으로 설계되었기 때문에 여전히 차를 몰고 출근한다; 형평성을 중시하는 유권자도 다른 사람들에게 불리한 제도로부터 여전히 혜택을 받으며, 종종 그것을 충분히 인식하지 못한 채 그러한다.

grammarElements.text: A consumer who cares about the environment
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: because the city was designed around cars
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (수동태)

grammarElements.text: a voter who values equity still benefits from arrangements that disadvantage others
grammarElements.label: 세미콜론 뒤 병렬 구조 (관계절 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: often without being fully aware of it
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (동명사 포함)

vocabulary.example: True equity requires addressing systemic disadvantages.
Existing arrangements often favor those already in power.
Some policies disadvantage minorities.

expressions.usage: 의도와 행동의 괴리를 still로 강조하며, because로 구조적 이유를 제시한다

expressions.phrase: without being fully aware of it
expressions.usage: 무의식적 참여를 부드럽게 지적하는 표현

patterns.pattern: A [person] who [values X] still [contradictory action] because [structural reason]; a [person] who [values Y] still [contradictory action], often without [awareness]
patterns.explanation: 세미콜론으로 연결된 두 개의 병렬 사례를 통해 가치와 행동의 구조적 괴리를 보여주는 패턴. 각 사례가 개인의 의도와 시스템의 제약 사이의 긴장을 구체화한다.
examples: A teacher who believes in creativity still follows rigid curricula because the school was built around standardized testing; a doctor who values prevention still treats symptoms, often without time for deeper care.

A parent who values independence still overprotects because society was organized around risk avoidance; a manager who values innovation still enforces rules, often without recognizing the contradiction.

drillQuestions.question: A consumer who cares about the environment still _____ to work.
The city was designed around _____.
A voter who values equity still _____ from unfair arrangements.
"Without being fully aware of it" means without complete _____.

sentences.notes: 세미콜론으로 연결된 두 개의 병렬 사례가 이론적 주장을 구체적으로 예증한다. "still"이 두 절 모두에서 의도와 행동의 괴리를 강조한다. "without being fully aware of it"이 비난이 아닌 구조적 인식의 한계를 부드럽게 지적한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A consumer who cares about the environment
still drives to work because the city was designed around cars
koreanAlignment.ko: 도시가 자동차 중심으로 설계되었기 때문에 여전히 차를 몰고 출근한다

koreanAlignment.en: a voter who values equity
still benefits from arrangements that disadvantage others
koreanAlignment.ko: 다른 사람들에게 불리한 제도로부터 여전히 혜택을 받으며

koreanAlignment.en: often without being fully aware of it
koreanAlignment.ko: 종종 그것을 충분히 인식하지 못한 채

pronunciationNotes: equity: /ˈekwəti/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "에퀴티"에 가깝다. qu를 /kw/로 발음.
disadvantage: /ˌdɪsədˈvæntɪdʒ/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "디서드밴티지"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Recognizing this tension is the starting point of honest moral thinking, because it replaces the comfortable fiction of personal purity with the harder question of collective responsibility.
sentences.translation: 이 긴장을 인식하는 것이 정직한 도덕적 사고의 출발점인데, 왜냐하면 그것은 개인적 순수함이라는 편안한 허구를 집단적 책임이라는 더 어려운 질문으로 대체하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: the starting point of honest moral thinking
grammarElements.label: 보어 (명사구)

grammarElements.text: because it replaces the comfortable fiction of personal purity with the harder question of collective responsibility
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (replace A with B)

vocabulary.example: The tension between ideals and reality drives moral reflection.
Personal purity is a comfortable fiction we tell ourselves.
Climate change is a matter of collective responsibility.

expressions.usage: 사람들이 편하게 믿지만 사실이 아닌 것을 비판적으로 지칭하는 표현
사고의 전환을 나타내는 동사 구문

patterns.pattern: [Gerund] is the starting point of [X], because it replaces [comfortable illusion] with [harder truth]
patterns.explanation: 동명사 주어로 행위를 명명하고, "the starting point"로 그 중요성을 선언한 뒤, because절에서 replace A with B 구문으로 사고의 전환을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Admitting ignorance is the starting point of genuine learning, because it replaces the comfortable illusion of knowing with the productive discomfort of inquiry.
Questioning habits is the starting point of personal growth, because it replaces the comfortable routine of autopilot with the harder practice of intentional living.

drillQuestions.question: Recognizing this tension is the _____ point of honest moral thinking.
It replaces the comfortable _____ of personal purity.
"Collective responsibility" means the responsibility of the _____.
"Replaces A with B" means to _____ A and put B in its place.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 에세이 전반부의 논증을 요약하는 전환점이다. "comfortable fiction"이라는 날카로운 표현이 자기 위안적 도덕관을 비판한다. replace A with B 구문이 사고의 전환을 명확히 한다. "personal purity"와 "collective responsibility"의 대비가 개인 윤리에서 구조적 윤리로의 전환을 구현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Recognizing this tension
is the starting point of honest moral thinking
because it replaces the comfortable fiction of personal purity
koreanAlignment.ko: 개인적 순수함이라는 편안한 허구를 대체하기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: with the harder question of collective responsibility

pronunciationNotes: purity: /ˈpjʊrəti/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "퓨어러티"에 가깝다. pu를 /pjʊ/로 발음.
responsibility: /rɪˌspɑːnsəˈbɪləti/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "리스판서빌러티"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: If the world we want cannot be built by individual virtue alone, then the conversation must turn to institutions — the rules, norms, and organizations through which societies make collective decisions.
sentences.translation: 우리가 원하는 세상이 개인의 미덕만으로는 건설될 수 없다면, 대화는 제도로 — 사회가 집단적 결정을 내리는 규칙, 규범, 그리고 조직으로 — 전환되어야 한다.

grammarElements.text: If the world we want cannot be built by individual virtue alone
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (수동태 + 관계절)

grammarElements.text: the rules, norms, and organizations through which societies make collective decisions
grammarElements.label: 대시 동격구 (관계절 포함 3중 병렬)

vocabulary.example: Individual virtue is necessary but not sufficient.
Strong institutions protect democratic values.
Social norms shape behavior more than laws.

expressions.usage: 논의의 방향 전환을 선언할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
alone으로 단일 수단의 불충분함을 강조한다

patterns.pattern: If [goal] cannot be [achieved] by [X] alone, then [the discussion] must turn to [Y] — [definition of Y]
patterns.explanation: If-then 조건문으로 개인적 접근의 한계를 인정하고, 제도적 접근으로의 전환을 논리적으로 요구하는 패턴. 대시 뒤에 핵심 용어의 정의를 부가한다.
examples: If public health cannot be secured by personal discipline alone, then policy must turn to regulation — the laws, standards, and enforcement mechanisms through which governments protect populations.
If education cannot be improved by teacher dedication alone, then reform must turn to systems — the curricula, funding models, and evaluation tools through which schools operate.

drillQuestions.question: The world we want cannot be built by individual _____ alone.
The conversation must turn to _____.
Institutions include rules, norms, and _____.
"Must turn to" means the shift is _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 구조적 전환점. If-then 구문으로 개인 윤리에서 제도 논의로의 필연적 전환을 선언한다. 대시 뒤의 동격구("the rules, norms, and organizations ~")가 "institutions"의 의미를 즉시 정의하여 독자의 이해를 돕는다.

koreanAlignment.en: If the world we want cannot be built
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 원하는 세상이 건설될 수 없다면

koreanAlignment.en: by individual virtue alone
then the conversation must turn to institutions
the rules, norms, and organizations
through which societies make collective decisions

pronunciationNotes: virtue: /ˈvɜːrtʃuː/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "버추"에 가깝다. tu를 /tʃuː/로 발음.
institutions: /ˌɪnstɪˈtuːʃənz/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "인스티튜션즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Good institutions do not require every participant to be selfless; they align incentives so that ordinary, self-interested behavior produces outcomes that are broadly acceptable.
sentences.translation: 좋은 제도는 모든 참여자가 이타적일 것을 요구하지 않는다; 좋은 제도는 인센티브를 조정하여 평범하고 자기 이익 추구적인 행동이 대체로 수용 가능한 결과를 생산하도록 한다.

grammarElements.text: so that ordinary, self-interested behavior produces outcomes that are broadly acceptable
grammarElements.label: so that 목적절 (관계절 포함)

vocabulary.example: Good systems do not depend on selfless people.
Aligning incentives can change behavior without coercion.
The outcome was broadly acceptable to most parties.

expressions.usage: 제도 설계에서 유인 체계를 맞추는 것을 설명하는 전문적 표현
경제학적 관점에서 인간의 기본적 행동 동기를 중립적으로 묘사하는 표현

patterns.pattern: Good [systems] do not require [ideal behavior]; they [design mechanism] so that [ordinary behavior] produces [acceptable outcomes]
patterns.explanation: 세미콜론으로 이상주의적 요구를 부정하고, 현실적 설계 원리를 제시하는 패턴. 제도 설계의 핵심 논리를 간결하게 전달한다.
examples: Good laws do not require every citizen to be ethical; they create consequences so that ordinary compliance produces a functioning society.
Good management does not require every employee to be passionate; it designs workflows so that routine effort produces quality output.

drillQuestions.question: Good institutions do not require every participant to be _____.
They align _____ so that ordinary behavior produces good outcomes.
"Self-interested behavior" means acting for one's own _____.
"Broadly acceptable" means acceptable to _____ people.

sentences.notes: 제도 설계의 핵심 원리를 간결하게 제시하는 문장. "do not require ... to be selfless"가 이상주의를 부정하고, "align incentives so that"이 현실적 대안을 제시한다. 세미콜론이 부정과 긍정의 두 명제를 효과적으로 연결한다. 경제학적 사고(인센티브 정렬)를 도덕 논의에 적용한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Good institutions do not require every participant to be selfless
koreanAlignment.ko: 좋은 제도는 모든 참여자가 이타적일 것을 요구하지 않는다

koreanAlignment.en: they align incentives
so that ordinary, self-interested behavior
produces outcomes that are broadly acceptable
koreanAlignment.ko: 대체로 수용 가능한 결과를 생산하도록 한다

pronunciationNotes: incentives: /ɪnˈsentɪvz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "인센티브즈"에 가깝다.
selfless: /ˈselfləs/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "셀플리스"에 가깝다. -less를 약하게 발음.

sentences.original: A well-designed tax system, for instance, does not depend on citizens voluntarily donating money to public services; it creates a structure in which contribution is expected and the burden is shared.
sentences.translation: 예를 들어, 잘 설계된 세금 체계는 시민이 공공 서비스에 자발적으로 돈을 기부하는 것에 의존하지 않는다; 그것은 기여가 기대되고 부담이 공유되는 구조를 만든다.

grammarElements.text: citizens voluntarily donating money to public services
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (동명사구)

grammarElements.text: it creates a structure in which contribution is expected and the burden is shared
grammarElements.label: 세미콜론 뒤 병렬절 (관계절 내 수동태 병렬)

vocabulary.example: A well-designed system minimizes the need for enforcement.
Few people would voluntarily pay more taxes.
The tax burden should be shared fairly.

expressions.usage: 앞서 제시한 원리의 구체적 사례를 도입하는 삽입구

expressions.phrase: contribution is expected and the burden is shared
expressions.usage: 수동태 병렬로 제도의 두 가지 특성을 간결히 기술한다

patterns.pattern: A well-designed [system], for instance, does not depend on [voluntary ideal behavior]; it creates a structure in which [obligation] is expected and [cost] is shared
patterns.explanation: 앞 문장의 추상적 원리를 구체적 사례(세금)로 예증하는 패턴. 세미콜론으로 부정(의존하지 않음)과 긍정(구조를 만듦)을 연결한다.
examples: A well-designed health system, for instance, does not depend on individuals researching every treatment; it creates a structure in which expert review is guaranteed and access is universal.
A well-designed education system, for instance, does not depend on parents teaching every subject; it creates a structure in which qualified instruction is provided and opportunity is equalized.

drillQuestions.question: A well-designed tax system does not depend on citizens _____ donating money.
It creates a structure in which contribution is _____.
The _____ is shared among all citizens.
"For instance" introduces a specific _____.

sentences.notes: "for instance"가 추상적 원리를 세금이라는 익숙한 사례로 구체화한다. 세미콜론 뒤의 "in which" 관계절 안에서 두 개의 수동태("contribution is expected and the burden is shared")가 병렬을 이루며 제도의 두 축을 제시한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A well-designed tax system, for instance
koreanAlignment.ko: 예를 들어, 잘 설계된 세금 체계는

koreanAlignment.en: does not depend on citizens voluntarily donating money to public services
koreanAlignment.ko: 시민이 공공 서비스에 자발적으로 돈을 기부하는 것에 의존하지 않는다

koreanAlignment.en: it creates a structure
in which contribution is expected and the burden is shared

pronunciationNotes: voluntarily: /ˈvɑːlənterəli/ — 첫 음절에 강세 (미국식). "발런테럴리"에 가깝다. 영국식은 세 번째 음절 강세.
burden: /ˈbɜːrdən/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "버든"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The same logic applies to environmental regulation, public education, health care, and every other domain where private interest and public good must be reconciled.
sentences.translation: 같은 논리가 환경 규제, 공교육, 의료, 그리고 사적 이익과 공공선이 조화되어야 하는 모든 다른 영역에 적용된다.

grammarElements.text: environmental regulation, public education, health care, and every other domain
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (4중 병렬)

grammarElements.text: where private interest and public good must be reconciled
grammarElements.label: 관계부사절 (수동태)

vocabulary.example: Environmental regulation protects shared resources.
Health care is a domain where compromise is essential.
Private interest and public good must be reconciled.

expressions.usage: 앞서 설명한 원리를 다른 영역으로 확장할 때 쓰는 전환 표현
제도 설계에서 핵심적으로 조화시켜야 할 두 요소를 병렬로 제시하는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: The same logic applies to [list of domains] and every other domain where [tension] must be reconciled
patterns.explanation: 앞의 구체적 사례에서 도출된 원리를 다른 영역으로 일반화하는 패턴. 4중 병렬과 "every other"로 보편성을 강조한다.
examples: The same logic applies to criminal justice, immigration, trade policy, and every other domain where individual rights and collective security must be reconciled.
The same logic applies to urban planning, transportation, housing, and every other domain where economic growth and environmental sustainability must be reconciled.

drillQuestions.question: The same logic applies to environmental regulation, public education, _____ care.
Private interest and public good must be _____.
"Every other domain" means _____ other areas where this is relevant.
"The same logic applies to" means the _____ principle works in other areas too.

sentences.notes: 세금 사례에서 도출된 원리를 다른 영역으로 확장하는 일반화 문장. "environmental regulation, public education, health care"의 구체적 나열에 "every other domain"을 추가해 보편적 적용을 주장한다. "must be reconciled"의 수동태가 이 과제의 필연성을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The same logic applies to
environmental regulation, public education, health care
and every other domain
where private interest and public good must be reconciled

pronunciationNotes: reconciled: /ˈrekənsaɪld/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "레컨사일드"에 가깝다.
regulation: /ˌreɡjəˈleɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "레귤레이션"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Building better institutions requires something that pure idealism often lacks: patience with imperfection, willingness to compromise, and the understanding that incremental progress, however unsatisfying, is usually more durable than dramatic revolution.
sentences.translation: 더 나은 제도를 건설하려면 순수한 이상주의에 종종 결여된 것이 필요하다: 불완전함에 대한 인내, 타협할 의향, 그리고 점진적 진보가 아무리 불만족스럽더라도 대개 극적인 혁명보다 더 지속적이라는 이해.

grammarElements.text: something that pure idealism often lacks
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: patience with imperfection, willingness to compromise, and the understanding that incremental progress, however unsatisfying, is usually more durable than dramatic revolution
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 동격 설명 (3중 병렬)

vocabulary.example: Pure idealism often ignores practical constraints.
Willingness to compromise is essential in democracy.
Incremental progress adds up over time.
Slow progress is more durable than sudden leaps.

expressions.usage: 완벽하지 않은 결과를 견딜 수 있는 능력을 가리키는 표현
양보의 부사절이 삽입되어 현실의 불만족을 인정하면서도 요점을 유지한다

patterns.pattern: [Gerund] requires something that [pure X] often lacks: [quality A], [quality B], and the understanding that [modest outcome], however [negative], is usually more [positive] than [dramatic alternative]
patterns.explanation: 콜론 뒤에 세 가지 필수 자질을 3중 병렬로 나열하며, 세 번째 항목이 가장 복잡한 명제(however 양보절 포함 비교급)인 패턴. 실용주의적 주장의 전형적 구조이다.
examples: Improving education requires something that enthusiasm alone often lacks: tolerance for slow results, openness to criticism, and the understanding that small gains, however invisible, are usually more lasting than sweeping reforms.

Sustaining relationships requires something that passion often lacks: acceptance of flaws, willingness to negotiate, and the understanding that stable affection, however ordinary, is usually more valuable than intense romance.

drillQuestions.question: Building better institutions requires something that pure _____ often lacks.
The three requirements are patience, willingness to compromise, and _____.
Incremental progress is usually more _____ than dramatic revolution.
"However unsatisfying" means even though it is _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 핵심 주장을 담은 문장. 콜론 뒤의 3중 병렬("patience ~ willingness ~ understanding")이 점층적으로 복잡해지며, 세 번째 항목에 "however unsatisfying"이라는 양보 삽입이 들어가 실용주의의 뉘앙스를 살린다. "pure idealism"과 "incremental progress"의 대비가 글 전체의 논지를 요약한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Building better institutions requires
something that pure idealism often lacks
patience with imperfection
willingness to compromise
and the understanding that incremental progress, however unsatisfying, is usually more durable than dramatic revolution
koreanAlignment.ko: 그리고 점진적 진보가 아무리 불만족스럽더라도 대개 극적인 혁명보다 더 지속적이라는 이해

pronunciationNotes: idealism: /aɪˈdiːəlɪzəm/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "아이디얼리즘"에 가깝다.
incremental: /ˌɪnkrəˈmentəl/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "인크러멘틀"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: History suggests that the societies that improve most reliably are not those that demand perfection but those that create feedback loops — elections, free press, independent courts — through which mistakes can be identified and corrected without tearing the whole system apart.
sentences.translation: 역사는 가장 안정적으로 개선되는 사회가 완벽을 요구하는 사회가 아니라 피드백 루프 — 선거, 언론의 자유, 독립적 법원 — 를 만들어 실수가 전체 시스템을 무너뜨리지 않고 확인되고 교정될 수 있는 사회임을 시사한다.

grammarElements.text: that the societies that improve most reliably
grammarElements.label: that 명사절의 주어 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: are not those that demand perfection but those that create feedback loops
grammarElements.label: not A but B 보어 (이중 관계절)

grammarElements.text: through which mistakes can be identified and corrected without tearing the whole system apart
grammarElements.label: 전치사 관계절 (수동태 + 동명사)

vocabulary.example: The best systems improve reliably, not spectacularly.
Feedback loops allow systems to self-correct.
Mistakes must first be identified before they can be fixed.

expressions.phrase: not those that demand perfection but those that create
expressions.meaning: 완벽을 요구하는 것이 아니라 ~을 만드는 것
expressions.usage: not A but B 구조로 두 유형의 사회를 대비하는 표현

expressions.phrase: without tearing the whole system apart
expressions.usage: 변화가 파괴 없이 이루어짐을 강조하는 표현

patterns.pattern: History suggests that [the best X] are not those that [demand ideal] but those that [create mechanism] — [examples] — through which [errors] can be [corrected] without [destruction]
patterns.explanation: 역사를 권위로 삼아, not A but B로 두 유형을 대비하고, 대시 삽입구로 구체적 메커니즘을 예시한 뒤, 전치사 관계절로 그 기능을 설명하는 복합 패턴.
examples: History suggests that the organizations that survive longest are not those that avoid all errors but those that build review processes — audits, evaluations, whistleblower protections — through which problems can be surfaced and addressed without collapsing the whole operation.

History suggests that the economies that grow most steadily are not those that eliminate risk but those that create safety nets — insurance, regulation, social programs — through which failures can be absorbed without destabilizing the entire market.

drillQuestions.question: History suggests that societies improve most reliably by creating feedback _____.
Examples of feedback loops include elections, free press, and independent _____.
Mistakes can be identified and _____ without destroying the system.
"Tearing the whole system apart" means _____ the system.

sentences.notes: 글에서 가장 구조적으로 복잡한 문장 중 하나. "History suggests"가 권위를 부여하고, "not those that ~ but those that ~"의 이중 관계절이 두 유형의 사회를 대비한다. 대시 삽입구("elections, free press, independent courts")가 추상적 "feedback loops"를 즉시 구체화한다. "without tearing ~ apart"가 점진적 교정의 핵심 가치를 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: History suggests that
the societies that improve most reliably
are not those that demand perfection
but those that create feedback loops
elections, free press, independent courts
through which mistakes can be identified and corrected without tearing the whole system apart
koreanAlignment.ko: 실수가 전체 시스템을 무너뜨리지 않고 확인되고 교정될 수 있는

pronunciationNotes: reliably: /rɪˈlaɪəbli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "릴라이어블리"에 가깝다.
identified: /aɪˈdentɪfaɪd/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "아이덴티파이드"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The world we want to build, then, is not a utopia but a learning system: one that is honest about its failures, responsive to evidence, and committed to the principle that every generation should leave conditions slightly better than it found them.
sentences.translation: 그렇다면, 우리가 건설하고자 하는 세상은 유토피아가 아니라 학습 시스템이다: 자신의 실패에 대해 정직하고, 증거에 반응하며, 모든 세대가 자신이 발견한 것보다 약간 더 나은 상태로 조건을 남겨야 한다는 원칙에 헌신하는 시스템.

grammarElements.text: The world we want to build
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: not a utopia but a learning system
grammarElements.label: 보어 (not A but B)

grammarElements.text: one that is honest about its failures, responsive to evidence, and committed to the principle that every generation should leave conditions slightly better than it found them
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 동격구 (관계절 내 3중 병렬 + that절)

vocabulary.example: A utopia is an imagined perfect society.
A good system is responsive to new evidence.
We must be committed to the principle of fairness.

expressions.phrase: not a utopia but a learning system
expressions.usage: not A but B로 이상적 목표를 현실적 목표로 재정의하는 핵심 표현

expressions.phrase: leave conditions slightly better than it found them
expressions.meaning: 자신이 발견한 것보다 약간 더 나은 상태로 조건을 남기다
expressions.usage: 세대 간 책임의 원칙을 간결하게 표현하는 격언적 표현

patterns.pattern: The [goal], then, is not [utopia] but [realistic alternative]: one that is [A], [B], and committed to the principle that [modest inter-generational obligation]
patterns.explanation: "then"으로 논리적 결론을 도출하고, not A but B로 이상을 재정의한 뒤, 콜론 뒤에 3중 병렬로 그 특성을 구체화하는 패턴. 에세이의 결론적 주장을 담는다.
examples: The education we want, then, is not a perfect curriculum but an adaptive process: one that is honest about what works, responsive to student needs, and committed to the principle that every cohort should be better prepared than the last.

The democracy we want, then, is not a flawless system but a self-correcting one: one that is transparent about its errors, responsive to citizens, and committed to the principle that each election should make representation slightly more fair.

drillQuestions.question: The world we want to build is not a _____ but a learning system.
A learning system is honest about its _____.
Every generation should leave conditions slightly _____ than it found them.
"Then" in this sentence signals a logical _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 핵심 결론을 선언하는 문장. "then"이 앞선 모든 논증으로부터의 논리적 귀결임을 명시한다. "not a utopia but a learning system"이 에세이 전체의 논지를 한 구절에 압축한다. 콜론 뒤의 3중 병렬이 "learning system"의 세 가지 특성을 제시하며, "leave conditions slightly better"가 겸손하지만 강력한 원칙을 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The world we want to build, then
is not a utopia but a learning system
one that is honest about its failures
responsive to evidence
and committed to the principle that every generation should leave conditions slightly better than it found them
koreanAlignment.ko: 모든 세대가 자신이 발견한 것보다 약간 더 나은 상태로 조건을 남겨야 한다는 원칙에 헌신하는

pronunciationNotes: utopia: /juːˈtoʊpiə/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "유토우피어"에 가깝다.
committed: /kəˈmɪtɪd/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "커미티드"에 가깝다. t가 두 번.

sentences.original: That principle sounds modest, but consistently applied over decades, it produces transformations that no single visionary could have planned.
sentences.translation: 그 원칙은 소박하게 들리지만, 수십 년에 걸쳐 일관되게 적용되면 어떤 단일 선각자도 계획할 수 없었을 변혁을 만들어 낸다.

grammarElements.text: it produces transformations that no single visionary could have planned
grammarElements.label: 주절 (관계절 + 가정법 과거완료)

vocabulary.example: The proposal sounds modest but its impact is enormous.
Consistently applied effort produces great results.
No single visionary could have predicted this transformation.
Small consistent efforts produce great transformations.

expressions.usage: 감각동사 sound + 형용사 구조로 겉보기 인상을 표현한다
분사구문이 축약된 형태. "when/if it is consistently applied"의 의미

patterns.pattern: That [principle] sounds [modest/small], but [consistently applied over time], it produces [transformations] that no [single agent] could have [planned]
patterns.explanation: 소박한 원칙의 겉보기 인상과 장기적 결과의 극적 대비를 but으로 연결하는 패턴. 가정법 과거완료("could have planned")가 그 결과의 예측 불가능성을 강조한다.
examples: That habit sounds trivial, but consistently practiced over years, it produces changes that no personal trainer could have engineered.
That policy sounds boring, but faithfully maintained over generations, it produces prosperity that no economic genius could have designed.

drillQuestions.question: That principle sounds _____, but it produces great transformations.
Consistently applied over _____, it produces transformations.
No single _____ could have planned such transformations.
"Could have planned" is in the _____ tense.

sentences.notes: 짧지만 강력한 수사적 문장. "sounds modest"와 "produces transformations"의 극적 대비가 점진주의의 역설적 힘을 보여준다. "consistently applied over decades"라는 분사구문이 시간적 조건을 간결하게 제시한다. "no single visionary could have planned"의 가정법 과거완료가 겸손함과 야심의 조화를 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: That principle sounds modest
but consistently applied over decades
it produces transformations
that no single visionary could have planned
koreanAlignment.ko: 어떤 단일 선각자도 계획할 수 없었을

pronunciationNotes: visionary: /ˈvɪʒəneri/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "비저너리"에 가깝다. s를 /ʒ/로 발음.
transformations: /ˌtrænsfərˈmeɪʃənz/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "트랜스퍼메이션즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The question is not whether we can imagine a perfect world — anyone can do that — but whether we are willing to do the patient, unglamorous work of making the actual world a little more like the one we imagine.
sentences.translation: 질문은 우리가 완벽한 세상을 상상할 수 있느냐가 아니다 — 누구나 그것은 할 수 있다 — 실제 세상을 우리가 상상하는 세상에 조금 더 가깝게 만드는 인내심 있고 화려하지 않은 작업을 할 의향이 있느냐이다.

grammarElements.text: not whether we can imagine a perfect world
grammarElements.label: 보어 (부정 whether절)

grammarElements.text: but whether we are willing to do the patient, unglamorous work of making the actual world a little more like the one we imagine
grammarElements.label: not A but B의 B (whether절 + 동명사구 + 관계절)

vocabulary.example: Real progress requires unglamorous daily effort.
Patient effort produces lasting change.
Anyone can imagine a perfect world.

expressions.phrase: the question is not whether A but whether B
expressions.usage: not A but B를 whether절과 결합한 고급 수사적 구문. 에세이의 핵심 질문을 재구성한다
두 형용사가 이상주의적 기대와 대비되는 현실적 노력을 묘사한다

patterns.pattern: The question is not whether [easy vision] — anyone can do that — but whether [we are willing to do the hard work of making reality closer to the vision]
patterns.explanation: 에세이의 결론을 수사적 질문으로 마무리하는 패턴. 대시 삽입구로 쉬운 부분을 일축하고, but 뒤에 진짜 도전을 제시한다. 제목의 질문을 변형하여 되돌려줌으로써 원형 구조를 완성한다.
examples: The question is not whether we can dream of equality — anyone can do that — but whether we are willing to do the tedious, incremental work of dismantling the systems that produce inequality.
The question is not whether we can describe a healthy planet — anyone can do that — but whether we are willing to do the costly, inconvenient work of changing how we live every day.

drillQuestions.question: The question is not whether we can _____ a perfect world.
Anyone can do that — but are we willing to do the _____ work?
The work involves making the actual world more like the one we _____.
This final sentence echoes the _____ of the essay.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 결론이자 수사적 절정. 제목("What Kind of World Do We Want to Build?")의 질문을 변형하여 되돌려줌으로써 원형 구조를 완성한다. "anyone can do that"이라는 대시 삽입구가 상상의 쉬움을 일축한다. "patient, unglamorous work"가 에세이 전체의 논지 — 점진적, 실용적, 겸손한 노력 — 를 두 단어에 압축한다. "a little more like the one we imagine"이 완벽이 아닌 근접을 목표로 삼는 현실적 야심을 표현한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The question is not whether we can imagine a perfect world
koreanAlignment.ko: 질문은 우리가 완벽한 세상을 상상할 수 있느냐가 아니다

koreanAlignment.en: anyone can do that
but whether we are willing to do
the patient, unglamorous work
of making the actual world a little more like the one we imagine
koreanAlignment.ko: 실제 세상을 우리가 상상하는 세상에 조금 더 가깝게 만드는

pronunciationNotes: unglamorous: /ʌnˈɡlæmərəs/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "언글래머러스"에 가깝다. un- 접두사가 "화려하지 않은"을 뜻한다.
imagine: /ɪˈmædʒɪn/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "이매진"에 가깝다.
